top_strategic.title: Brand Safety Engine for Sports Streaming
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Every sports streamer fears misattribution and brand safety disasters. Offer a white-label real-time classifier that flags brand appearances, validates them against streamer's approved list, and auto-blocks or flags unsafe associations. Your 49-rule corpus becomes the gold standard; you own the SLA for accuracy.

counterargument.argument: Streamers already have legal+compliance teams reviewing brand safety — this adds a dependency on your classifier's recall, not their existing workflows.
counterargument.risk: If your classifier misses a brand appearance (false negative), the streamer's compliance team still catches it, but now they distrust your system and stop using it. If you over-flag (false positive), you create alert fatigue and the streamer disables the feature. Either way, you've inserted yourself into a critical safety path without solving the core problem — human review is still required.

top_strategic.title: Venue Operating System: Sponsor Dashboard
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Embed the classifier into venue software. Real-time sponsor dashboard: 'Your brand appeared 47 times in last 3 tournaments, 23 of those in broadcast-grade moments.' Venues become the distribution layer for your classifier. They upsell sponsors on data; you get higher accuracy feedback and recurring venue licensing.

counterargument.argument: Venues care about sponsorship ROI, not brand appearance counts — they want attribution tied to viewer engagement or sales lift, which your classifier doesn't measure.
counterargument.risk: You're embedding a classifier into venue software that answers 'how many times did this brand appear?' but venues actually need 'did this appearance drive sponsor value?' Without converting raw detections into business metrics, adoption stalls and venues rip out the integration. If venues start making sponsorship renewal decisions based on your appearance counts alone, you're liable for inflated or deflated metrics that affect their revenue.

top_strategic.title: Taxonomy Licensing to Competing Sports Data Vendors
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Tennis/padel/squash data companies are solving your old problem — they built generic classifiers. License them the pickleball taxonomy structure, the rule-writing methodology, and crucially: your decision-loop framework (classifier→human→update→retrain). They pay per sport instance; each sport improves your core IP.

counterargument.argument: Competing sports data vendors already have domain expertise and customer relationships — licensing your taxonomy doesn't guarantee they'll use it correctly or maintain quality alignment.
counterargument.risk: If a tennis vendor licenses your rules but applies them loosely (different video quality, different rule interpretation), they'll produce bad classifications under your methodology name, damaging your brand. You also lose control over taxonomy evolution — they'll fork it, and now you have fragmented versions in the wild. Auditing their implementations will become expensive, and you have no leverage to force them to stay aligned.

top_strategic.title: Open-Source Classifier + Managed Service Upsell
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Release the 49-rule taxonomy and decision logic as Apache 2.0. Competing sports and venues adopt it freely. Monetize the managed tier: we host it, we retrain it monthly with your footage, we guarantee 95%+ accuracy. Free adoption → high switching costs → recurring SaaS revenue.

counterargument.argument: Open-sourcing the taxonomy invites rule contributions from low-quality actors, fragmenting the standard and degrading the managed service's value prop.
counterargument.risk: If you release 49 rules under Apache 2.0, someone will fork it, add 100 bad rules, and call it 'Pickle Classifier Pro.' Your managed service now competes against a free variant that might actually be more feature-complete (even if lower quality). Venues and brands won't pay for 'guaranteed 95%+ accuracy' when a free fork claims 98%. You also lose audit control — you can't enforce that commercial forks maintain your accuracy standards, creating liability if a bad fork is used for compliance decisions.

top_strategic.title: Crowdsourced Rule Expansion as a Moat
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Commission external sports leagues (badminton, pickleball in 15 countries, padel) to supply footage + human reviewers. They help you build region/sport variants of the 49-rule core. You own the aggregate decision data; they get localized classifier. Data moat deepens; you become the standard.

counterargument.argument: Crowdsourcing rule expansion introduces quality variance and dilutes ownership of the decision-making framework.
counterargument.risk: If badminton and padel leagues contribute rules but apply them inconsistently (different definition of 'visible brand' across sports), your aggregate training data becomes noisy. You claim ownership of the decision data, but if a padel league's rule causes false positives in pickleball videos, you're responsible for the fallout. Managing 15 regional variants also means you can't roll back a bad rule globally — you have to negotiate with each league. The 'moat' of aggregate decision data only works if all variants converge; if they diverge, you're managing a splintered taxonomy.

top_strategic.title: Brand Verification Marketplace
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Brands claim 'we appeared in 500 hours of pro pickleball content.' You become the auditor. Offer a verification badge: 'Verified by Pickle DaaS Classifier.' Brands pay for audit; pay-per-verified-appearance. Your rule set is the trust anchor; competitors can't replicate the decision corpus.

counterargument.argument: Brands are already claiming inflated sponsorship metrics; your classifier doesn't solve the fundamental misalignment between what brands claim and what they actually received.
counterargument.risk: If a brand pays for a 'verification badge' and your classifier certifies '500 hours of appearances,' but a competitor challenges the audit, you're now the arbiter of a revenue-affecting claim. False negatives (you miss real appearances) anger the brand; false positives (you count background blur as a brand) expose you to fraud allegations. You also create a perverse incentive for brands to push for overly permissive rules so their audit score is higher, further degrading classifier accuracy across all downstream users who trust the same rules.

top_data.title: Top 10 unknown brands account for 314 detections — high-leverage rule targets
top_data.evidence_summary: Most frequent unknowns: JOOLA(220): 'shirt chest'; 7(21): 'Signage on the fence in the background'; R&M(16): 'Top left corner of the video feed overla'; goomba(16): 'Top left corner of the video feed overla'; Unknown(9): 'worn by the primary player'; UG(9): 'Banner on the wall behind the court'. A rule for any of these immediately converts N detections from unknown to classified. Sort by frequency and research top 5.
top_data.recommended_action: For each top unknown: 1-call Haiku research ('what is X in the pickleball world?'); propose rule.

location_samples.7: Signage on the fence in the background
location_samples.R&M: Top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.goomba: Top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.UG: Banner on the wall behind the court
location_samples.R&M_fox_wifi_goomba: top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.R8M: Top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.2026-01-05: Top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.Foz_4_UG_Court: Signage on the wall behind the court.

counterargument.argument: Top unknowns like JOOLA (220 detections) may be concentrated in a single tournament or camera angle — writing rules for dataset-specific noise won't generalize.
counterargument.risk: If you call Haiku research and learn 'JOOLA is a paddle brand that appears on 20% of player gear,' you'll want to add a rule. But 220 detections across how many videos? If they're all from one tournament with one camera position, the rule won't fire in future tournaments. You'll also create a precedent that every brand appearing 16+ times gets a rule, leading to rule bloat. Without stratifying detections by video source, date, and camera angle, you can't distinguish signal from noise.

top_data.title: 7.4% of classifications are fallback (no rule match) — every % is a rule gap
top_data.evidence_summary: 340 detections use fallback classification vs 4261 that match a rule (92.6% rule coverage). Fallback is the classifier guessing — each one is a rule we haven't written yet.
top_data.recommended_action: Cluster fallback detections and promote the top clusters into new rules.

counterargument.argument: A 7.4% fallback rate may be acceptable — not every detection deserves a rule, and creating rules for rare edge cases increases false positives and maintenance burden.
counterargument.risk: If you aggressively promote fallback detections into new rules without understanding *why* they fell back, you'll add rules that match too broadly or contradict existing rules. This introduces silent drift — your rule count grows from 49 to 70+, but you haven't tested rule interaction effects. You also risk overfitting to this specific dataset; rules optimized for these 340 fallbacks may perform poorly on future footage. Migrations to a larger taxonomy are operationally expensive and break downstream dashboards expecting the original 49 rules.

top_data.title: 1 rules look right but you override them often
top_data.evidence_summary: DUDE CLOTHING: 1/2 decisions overridden (4 corpus fires). Sample note: "Probably something there. Keep looking out for underserved brands and try to build some confidence around it. It could be fun and rewarding." These rules pass the auto-verified bar but your manual review keeps correcting them — the classification they produce is technically legal but rarely what you actually want.
top_data.recommended_action: Adjust the rule defaults to match the override you keep making.

PSA: Oh, okay, this is cool. You actually have some versions of uploaded videos that I put in for manual AI analysis. Let's definitely classify these as much as possible, and there should be a lot more dat

top_data.title: 25 items in investigate queue read like real brands
top_data.evidence_summary: Likely real brands stuck waiting: PUSHIT; PKLR; PBS; PSA; PKLR; PBS. Their rationales mention product context (paddle, apparel, sponsor, etc.). If left unclassified, they inflate the unknown bucket and hide signal.
top_data.recommended_action: Auto-research each via web lookup, draft a proposed rule, ship for review.

top_data.title: 15 decisions override the classifier's proposed category — override-to-decision ratio = 20.3%
top_data.evidence_summary: Top override categories: {'broadcast_tech': 3, 'unknown': 2, 'court_surface': 2}. Top override values: {'medium': 9, 'high': 3, 'low': 2}. Every override is a signal the rule's default is slightly off. High override-to-decision ratio = classifier is running behind the taxonomy.
top_data.recommended_action: For override categories you repeat, update the rule's default — don't make yourself say it twice.

top_data.title: Your 'investigate' decisions cluster into 5 reasons — the classifier could route future ones automatically
top_data.evidence_summary: Clustering 30 investigate-notes via Haiku: Needs visual evidence to decide (e.g. BHP); Ambiguous category type (e.g. ODESSA); Needs classification refinement (e.g. PKLR); Future-value potential (e.g. R&M_foz_wifi_goomba); Emerging data source with untapped value (e.g. PSA). Each theme is a rule the classifier could apply itself: when a new brand matches theme X, auto-route to investigate with that rationale pre-filled.
top_data.recommended_action: Add a 'pre-route' step: before surfacing unknowns for your review, apply these themes and pre-fill the rationale + override_category.

themes.rationale: PO requires seeing pictures or video clips to make classification decisions rather than relying on metadata alone.
Brand identity unclear because it could be multiple things (brand vs. place vs. other), requiring context to disambiguate.
Entity is valid but requires distinguishing between different presentation formats (signage, banner, watermark, apparel) before proper categorization.
Not immediately necessary but could become valuable with enhanced data linkage or classification capabilities in the future.

themes.theme: Emerging data source with untapped value
themes.rationale: New data type (uploaded videos with overlay data) has potential for more comprehensive classification if additional resources are extracted.

top_data.title: 8 probable taxonomy merges would consolidate 911 items
top_data.evidence_summary: Candidate merges: headwear(1) + hat(1) → headwear (2 items); shoes(409) + shoe_brand(1) → shoes (410 items); apparel_bottom(2) + apparel_graphic(1) + clothing_brand(9) + apparel_top(22) + apparel(40) → apparel (74 items); drink_bottle(3) + water_bottle(1) → water_bottle (4 items); camera(45) + camera_equipment(1) → camera (46 items); watermark(1) + broadcast_tech(14) → broadcast_tech (15 items). These categories likely refer to the same concept under different names. Keeping them split fragments category analytics and confuses downstream consumers.
top_data.recommended_action: Pick one canonical name per merge group, run a migration on classified-corpus.json, bump taxonomy_version.

top_data.title: 5 brands are classified as multiple primary categories across clips
top_data.evidence_summary: Examples: PUSHIT: court_surface×5 + sponsor_signage×4 (of 10); DUNLOP: sponsor_signage×8 + ball×2 (of 10); Franklin: paddle×7 + ball×2 (of 9); PKLR: clothing_brand×6 + apparel_top×2 (of 9); Under Armour: apparel×4 + shoes×2 (of 6). When the same canonical_name gets different category_primary values on different clips, downstream aggregation double-counts or under-counts. The rule is either too loose or the brand legitimately sells in multiple categories.
top_data.recommended_action: For each: decide if the split is real (brand sells in both) or a rule bug. If real, add secondary-category convention; if bug, tighten the when_category clauses.

top_data.title: Haiku rescues 1 real brands from the top-15 unknown bucket
top_data.evidence_summary: Claude Haiku verdicts: {'real_brand': 1, 'noise': 8, 'new_category': 3}. Proposed as real brands: JOOLA. Each gets a recommended category — review and convert to rules.
top_data.recommended_action: Accept the real_brand verdicts, draft proposed-rules entries, ship for morning review.

verdicts.reasoning: JOOLA is a legitimate and prominent pickleball paddle/equipment manufacturer with significant market presence.
A single digit on fence signage is likely a court number, facility marking, or OCR artifact rather than a brand.
Appears consistently in video overlay corner, likely a broadcast or streaming platform watermark rather than a consumer brand.
Appears only in overlay corners paired with other metadata; likely a streaming software, Wi-Fi SSID, or system artifact.
Placeholder text indicating unidentifiable content; not a classifiable brand.
Appears on court/facility signage; likely a facility or venue identifier rather than a consumer sports brand.
Composite string mixing broadcast overlay elements (R&M), network info (wifi), and software (goomba); clear OCR or metadata artifact.
Appears in overlay corner; likely a variant/corruption of R&M broadcast watermark or system metadata.
A date string in video overlay is metadata or timestamp, not a brand.
Structured court/facility identifier combining location info and court number; venue or facility metadata.
A date string in video overlay is timestamp metadata, not a brand.
Capitalized variant of goomba appearing once in overlay; likely software or streaming platform artifact.

top_data.title: 2 approved rules fire almost never — cleanup candidates
top_data.evidence_summary: Of 49 approved rules, 2 fire ≤1 time against the corpus. 2 of those are flagged as revenue-relevant (not 'none'). Examples: CRBN, SELKIRK. Either the brand is absent from the corpus or the rule's aliases miss the actual spellings.
top_data.recommended_action: Review each dead rule — tighten aliases, merge with a sibling, or delete.

all_findings.recommended_action: Review each dead rule — tighten aliases, merge with a sibling, or delete.

all_findings.title: Top 5 rules carry 3861 of 4261 classifier fires
all_findings.evidence_summary: Stars: JOOLA (3295 fires), NIKE (319 fires), PICKLEBALL (116 fires), HEAD (74 fires), ADIDAS (57 fires). These rules fire often AND almost never get overridden — they're doing the heavy lifting. Corpus has 4601 total detections, 92.6% coverage.
all_findings.recommended_action: Don't touch these. Use them as templates for new brand rules.

all_findings.title: 13 taxonomy categories have ≤3 members — noise or genuine rarity?
all_findings.evidence_summary: Singleton-ish categories: hat(1), apparel_graphic(1), headwear(1), camera_equipment(1), watermark(1), water_bottle(1), shoe_brand(1), tech_accessory(1), other(2), apparel_bottom(2). A new category should only exist if it will sustain ≥5 detections. Below that, it's either a mistype (same concept as another category) or too rare to sell against.
all_findings.recommended_action: For each singleton: delete, merge into a bigger category, or mark 'archive'.

all_findings.title: 5 brands are classified as multiple primary categories across clips
all_findings.evidence_summary: Examples: PUSHIT: court_surface×5 + sponsor_signage×4 (of 10); DUNLOP: sponsor_signage×8 + ball×2 (of 10); Franklin: paddle×7 + ball×2 (of 9); PKLR: clothing_brand×6 + apparel_top×2 (of 9); Under Armour: apparel×4 + shoes×2 (of 6). all_findings.title: Unknown bucket holds 319 items (6.9% of all classifications)
all_findings.evidence_summary: The 'unknown' primary category has 319 items out of 4601 total — 6.9%. Every unknown is either a brand we haven't written a rule for yet or noise (OCR artifacts, Wi-Fi SSIDs, single letters on fences). The Unknown Surfer researcher digs into WHICH.
all_findings.recommended_action: Run Unknown Surfer; auto-research top clusters; convert winners to rules.

all_findings.title: You write decisions at 3 speeds: 33 approve, 38 investigate, 3 reject
all_findings.evidence_summary: Across 74 total decisions: {'approve': 33, 'investigate': 38, 'reject': 3}. Revenue tags: {'none': 21, 'medium': 21, 'high': 10, 'low': 22}. 48/74 decisions carry a free-text note (avg 192 chars). The notes are where the policy lives — any future predictor has to read them, not just the action.
all_findings.recommended_action: Use this distribution as the prior — a predictor that guesses 'approve' every time scores the base rate.

all_findings.title: 5 brands changed decision across review cycles — you're second-guessing yourself
all_findings.evidence_summary: Examples: PUSHIT: investigate → approve; Foz Bullet UG 4K: investigate → approve; PBS: investigate → investigate → approve; BHP: investigate → approve; DUDE CLOTHING: investigate → approve. When the same brand flips approve/investigate/reject across reviews, the decision isn't stable — either the evidence changed, or the policy is still forming. These brands deserve a note on WHY the flip.
all_findings.recommended_action: Surface these on the morning report with both rationales so you can reconcile.

Venues become the distribution layer for your classifier. They upsell sponsors on data; you get higher accuracy feedback and recurring venue licensing.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: platform_play play

counterargument.argument: Venues care about sponsorship ROI, not brand appearance counts — they want attribution tied to viewer engagement or sales lift, which your classifier doesn't measure.
counterargument.risk: You're embedding a classifier into venue software that answers 'how many times did this brand appear?' but venues actually need 'did this appearance drive sponsor value?' Without converting raw detections into business metrics, adoption stalls and venues rip out the integration. If venues start making sponsorship renewal decisions based on your appearance counts alone, you're liable for inflated or deflated metrics that affect their revenue.

all_findings.title: Taxonomy Licensing to Competing Sports Data Vendors
all_findings.evidence_summary: Tennis/padel/squash data companies are solving your old problem — they built generic classifiers. License them the pickleball taxonomy structure, the rule-writing methodology, and crucially: your decision-loop framework (classifier→human→update→retrain). They pay per sport instance; each sport improves your core IP.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: licensing play

counterargument.argument: Competing sports data vendors already have domain expertise and customer relationships — licensing your taxonomy doesn't guarantee they'll use it correctly or maintain quality alignment.
counterargument.risk: If a tennis vendor licenses your rules but applies them loosely (different video quality, different rule interpretation), they'll produce bad classifications under your methodology name, damaging your brand. You also lose control over taxonomy evolution — they'll fork it, and now you have fragmented versions in the wild. Auditing their implementations will become expensive, and you have no leverage to force them to stay aligned.

all_findings.title: Open-Source Classifier + Managed Service Upsell
all_findings.evidence_summary: Release the 49-rule taxonomy and decision logic as Apache 2.0. Competing sports and venues adopt it freely. Monetize the managed tier: we host it, we retrain it monthly with your footage, we guarantee 95%+ accuracy. Free adoption → high switching costs → recurring SaaS revenue.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: tool_spinoff play

counterargument.argument: Open-sourcing the taxonomy invites rule contributions from low-quality actors, fragmenting the standard and degrading the managed service's value prop.
counterargument.risk: If you release 49 rules under Apache 2.0, someone will fork it, add 100 bad rules, and call it 'Pickle Classifier Pro.' Your managed service now competes against a free variant that might actually be more feature-complete (even if lower quality). Venues and brands won't pay for 'guaranteed 95%+ accuracy' when a free fork claims 98%. You also lose audit control — you can't enforce that commercial forks maintain your accuracy standards, creating liability if a bad fork is used for compliance decisions.

all_findings.title: Crowdsourced Rule Expansion as a Moat
all_findings.evidence_summary: Commission external sports leagues (badminton, pickleball in 15 countries, padel) to supply footage + human reviewers. They help you build region/sport variants of the 49-rule core. You own the aggregate decision data; they get localized classifier. Data moat deepens; you become the standard.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: crowdsource play

counterargument.argument: Crowdsourcing rule expansion introduces quality variance and dilutes ownership of the decision-making framework.
counterargument.risk: If badminton and padel leagues contribute rules but apply them inconsistently (different definition of 'visible brand' across sports), your aggregate training data becomes noisy. You claim ownership of the decision data, but if a padel league's rule causes false positives in pickleball videos, you're responsible for the fallout. Managing 15 regional variants also means you can't roll back a bad rule globally — you have to negotiate with each league. The 'moat' of aggregate decision data only works if all variants converge; if they diverge, you're managing a splintered taxonomy.

all_findings.title: Brand Verification Marketplace
all_findings.evidence_summary: Brands claim 'we appeared in 500 hours of pro pickleball content.' You become the auditor. Offer a verification badge: 'Verified by Pickle DaaS Classifier.' Brands pay for audit; pay-per-verified-appearance. Your rule set is the trust anchor; competitors can't replicate the decision corpus.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: trust_play play

counterargument.argument: Brands are already claiming inflated sponsorship metrics; your classifier doesn't solve the fundamental misalignment between what brands claim and what they actually received.
counterargument.risk: If a brand pays for a 'verification badge' and your classifier certifies '500 hours of appearances,' but a competitor challenges the audit, you're now the arbiter of a revenue-affecting claim. False negatives (you miss real appearances) anger the brand; false positives (you count background blur as a brand) expose you to fraud allegations. You also create a perverse incentive for brands to push for overly permissive rules so their audit score is higher, further degrading classifier accuracy across all downstream users who trust the same rules.

all_findings.title: Brand Intelligence Futures Market
all_findings.evidence_summary: Brands want to know: which venues, which players, which moments will give them the most visibility THIS QUARTER? Build a predictive layer on top of your classifier that forecasts brand-exposure value. Sell to brands and sponsors as a decision tool for where to spend partnership dollars. Your rules become pricing intel.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: media_play play

all_findings.title: Reverse Brand Intelligence: Competitive Espionage as a Service
all_findings.evidence_summary: Run your classifier on competitor footage (public YouTube, Twitch, Instagram). Sell 'Brand Presence Reports' to sponsors: 'JOOLA appeared 3x in pro matches last month, but Selkirk 8x. Here's the breakdown by venue, player, broadcast value.' Your rule set lets you see what generalist competitors miss.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: partnership_flip play

counterarguments.title: Streamers already have legal+compliance teams reviewing brand safety — this adds a dependency on your classifier's recall, not their existing workflows.
counterarguments.target_title: Brand Safety Engine for Sports Streaming
counterarguments.risk: If your classifier misses a brand appearance (false negative), the streamer's compliance team still catches it, but now they distrust your system and stop using it. If you over-flag (false positive), you create alert fatigue and the streamer disables the feature. Either way, you've inserted yourself into a critical safety path without solving the core problem — human review is still required.

counterarguments.title: Venues care about sponsorship ROI, not brand appearance counts — they want attribution tied to viewer engagement or sales lift, which your classifier doesn't measure.
counterarguments.risk: You're embedding a classifier into venue software that answers 'how many times did this brand appear?' but venues actually need 'did this appearance drive sponsor value?' Without converting raw detections into business metrics, adoption stalls and venues rip out the integration. If venues start making sponsorship renewal decisions based on your appearance counts alone, you're liable for inflated or deflated metrics that affect their revenue.

counterarguments.title: Competing sports data vendors already have domain expertise and customer relationships — licensing your taxonomy doesn't guarantee they'll use it correctly or maintain quality alignment.
counterarguments.target_title: Taxonomy Licensing to Competing Sports Data Vendors
counterarguments.risk: If a tennis vendor licenses your rules but applies them loosely (different video quality, different rule interpretation), they'll produce bad classifications under your methodology name, damaging your brand. You also lose control over taxonomy evolution — they'll fork it, and now you have fragmented versions in the wild. Auditing their implementations will become expensive, and you have no leverage to force them to stay aligned.

counterarguments.title: A 7.4% fallback rate may be acceptable — not every detection deserves a rule, and creating rules for rare edge cases increases false positives and maintenance burden.
counterarguments.target_title: 7.4% of classifications are fallback (no rule match) — every % is a rule gap
counterarguments.risk: If you aggressively promote fallback detections into new rules without understanding *why* they fell back, you'll add rules that match too broadly or contradict existing rules. This introduces silent drift — your rule count grows from 49 to 70+, but you haven't tested rule interaction effects. You also risk overfitting to this specific dataset; rules optimized for these 340 fallbacks may perform poorly on future footage. Migrations to a larger taxonomy are operationally expensive and break downstream dashboards expecting the original 49 rules.

counterarguments.title: Top unknowns like JOOLA (220 detections) may be concentrated in a single tournament or camera angle — writing rules for dataset-specific noise won't generalize.
counterarguments.target_title: Top 10 unknown brands account for 314 detections — high-leverage rule targets
counterarguments.risk: If you call Haiku research and learn 'JOOLA is a paddle brand that appears on 20% of player gear,' you'll want to add a rule. But 220 detections across how many videos? If they're all from one tournament with one camera position, the rule won't fire in future tournaments. You'll also create a precedent that every brand appearing 16+ times gets a rule, leading to rule bloat. Without stratifying detections by video source, date, and camera angle, you can't distinguish signal from noise.

counterarguments.title: Open-sourcing the taxonomy invites rule contributions from low-quality actors, fragmenting the standard and degrading the managed service's value prop.
counterarguments.risk: If you release 49 rules under Apache 2.0, someone will fork it, add 100 bad rules, and call it 'Pickle Classifier Pro.' Your managed service now competes against a free variant that might actually be more feature-complete (even if lower quality). Venues and brands won't pay for 'guaranteed 95%+ accuracy' when a free fork claims 98%. You also lose audit control — you can't enforce that commercial forks maintain your accuracy standards, creating liability if a bad fork is used for compliance decisions.

counterarguments.title: Crowdsourcing rule expansion introduces quality variance and dilutes ownership of the decision-making framework.
counterarguments.target_title: Crowdsourced Rule Expansion as a Moat
counterarguments.risk: If badminton and padel leagues contribute rules but apply them inconsistently (different definition of 'visible brand' across sports), your aggregate training data becomes noisy. You claim ownership of the decision data, but if a padel league's rule causes false positives in pickleball videos, you're responsible for the fallout. Managing 15 regional variants also means you can't roll back a bad rule globally — you have to negotiate with each league. The 'moat' of aggregate decision data only works if all variants converge; if they diverge, you're managing a splintered taxonomy.

False negatives (you miss real appearances) anger the brand; false positives (you count background blur as a brand) expose you to fraud allegations. You also create a perverse incentive for brands to push for overly permissive rules so their audit score is higher, further degrading classifier accuracy across all downstream users who trust the same rules.
